narrative: Your undergraduate GPA of 2.80 stood out as the biggest factor pushing the model's prediction toward expecting you to struggle on the bar exam, since it falls notably below the 3.24 average among students who passed on their first try. Next in influence was your first-year grade decile of 3, which again signaled lower early academic performance compared to the typical 6th decile for successful bar takers, reinforcing the model's concerns about your foundational strengths. While your LSAT score of 40 was a bright spot—actually above the 37 average for those who passed and pulling the prediction in a more favorable direction— it wasn't enough to offset those other key academic signals. Overall, the model estimated a 67% chance you'd fail the bar on your first attempt, which is why your law school application was rejected; the admissions process prioritizes applicants predicted to pass with high confidence to maintain strong bar passage rates. Your upper-middle family income aligns well with many who succeed, and planning full-time study matches the norm for passers, but those didn't carry as much weight here. Compared to typical accepted students, your profile shows solid potential in standardized testing and background support, but the early grades and undergrad record deviated enough to tip the scales against you. This isn't a judgment on your potential or determination—models like this focus narrowly on patterns from past bar outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT if possible to strengthen that positive influence, or target programs with bridge courses or bar prep support that could address GPA concerns. Building a stronger recent academic record through post-bac courses or a master's might also help recalibrate future predictions. Reach out to admissions counselors for feedback on holistic reviews, as some schools weigh personal stories beyond these metrics. With targeted improvements, you can reshape how models—and schools—see your path to the bar.
